title: Why a Single Perceptron Can't Learn XOR
concept: A single perceptron divides its world with exactly one straight line; XOR's true points and false points can't be separated by any single straight line, so no single perceptron can ever learn it — a property called linear separability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: For an XOR gate, which of the following pairs produces a FALSE output?
exercises.explain: Right — (1,1) is a matching pair, so XOR says false.

cards.title: Draw one line to split the room
cards.body: Imagine kids standing at four spots in a room based on two yes/no answers. Your job: draw ONE straight line on the floor so every "yes" kid ends up on one side and every "no" kid on the other. Easy for some patterns... impossible for others.

cards.title: Linear separability
cards.body: A perceptron makes decisions by drawing exactly one straight line (or, with more inputs, a flat plane) through its world. If a single straight line can put every "true" point on one side and every "false" point on the other, the pattern is linearly separable — and a perceptron can learn it.

cards.title: One slice of pizza
cards.body: A perceptron can only make one cut across the pizza, like a single knife stroke straight through the middle. AND and OR are patterns you CAN separate with one cut. XOR is a checkerboard pattern that no single straight slice can ever untangle.

cards.title: Plotting XOR on a graph
cards.body:
Plot the four XOR points on a graph, A on one axis and B on the other:
(0,0) → false  (1,1) → false — opposite CORNERS
(0,1) → true  (1,0) → true — ALSO opposite corners
The two true points sit diagonal from each other, and so do the two false points. No single straight line can fence off both true corners from both false corners at once.

cards.title: It's not a training problem
cards.body: You might think XOR just needs more practice, a better learning rate, or more training rounds. It doesn't matter how long you train a single perceptron on XOR — no amount of adjusting one straight line's position or angle will ever separate those diagonal points. The limitation is geometric, not a lack of effort.

cards.title: AND/OR vs. XOR
cards.body: Why one gate is easy and the other isn't:

left.text:
True points cluster together
ONE line separates them

right.text:
True points sit on opposite corners
NO single line works

exercises.prompt: Why can a single perceptron learn AND but not XOR?

options.text: AND's true/false points can be split by one line; XOR's can't
AND has fewer inputs than XOR
Perceptrons can only learn gates with even outputs

exercises.explain: Exactly — it all comes down to whether one straight line can separate the true points from the false points.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If you plot XOR's four input pairs on a graph, the two 'true' points and two 'false' points each sit on opposite corners.
exercises.explain: True! That diagonal arrangement is exactly why no single straight line can separate them.

exercises.sentence: A pattern that CAN be split by one straight line is called ___.
exercises.explain: Linearly separable patterns — like AND and OR — are exactly what a single perceptron is capable of learning.

exercises.prompt: Which gate pattern is linearly separable — solvable with just ONE straight line?
exercises.explain: OR's true points cluster on one side, so one line separates them easily. XOR's don't.

exercises.prompt: An engineer trains a single perceptron on XOR for 10,000 rounds, tweaking its weights every time. It still gets roughly half the answers wrong no matter how long training runs. What's really going on?

options.text: The problem isn't training time — XOR simply isn't linearly separable, so no single straight line (and therefore no single perceptron) can ever separate it
The perceptron needs a faster computer to find the right line
The perceptron just needs a bigger training dataset
XOR gates are too new for perceptrons to understand

exercises.explain: Right. This isn't a bug you can train away — it's a fundamental geometric wall. Next: a very real book proved this wall exists, and the whole field felt it.
